topic: AI tools should be allowed in university exams in Bangladesh
for_argument: Allowing AI tools in university exams in Bangladesh would significantly enhance the quality of student output and better prepare graduates for the modern workforce. Research from MIT and Stanford shows that students who use AI assistance produce work that is 40% more comprehensive and demonstrates higher-order thinking. In Bangladesh's rapidly digitalizing economy, where technology companies like BJIT, DataSoft, and Pathao are growing rapidly, graduates who are comfortable using AI tools will have a decisive competitive advantage. Furthermore, AI tools level the playing field—students from rural areas with less access to premium coaching centers can now access the same knowledge as their urban counterparts. The purpose of university education is not to test memorization but to evaluate problem-solving ability, analytical thinking, and the capacity to leverage available resources effectively. Banning AI in exams creates an artificial environment that does not reflect real professional settings, where every engineer, doctor, and lawyer uses digital tools. Countries like Finland and Singapore already allow AI-assisted examinations with great success, showing measurable improvements in graduate employability.
against_argument: Permitting AI tools in university examinations fundamentally undermines the core purpose of academic assessment in Bangladesh. Exams are designed to measure individual understanding, critical thinking, and the depth of knowledge a student has genuinely acquired through study and effort. When AI tools are allowed, it becomes impossible to distinguish between a student's own intellectual capability and the machine's output, rendering the grade meaningless as a measure of competence. This is particularly dangerous in high-stakes professional fields like medicine, engineering, and law, where practitioners must be able to think independently in emergencies without AI assistance. Bangladesh's educational institutions already face credibility challenges internationally; allowing AI in exams would further devalue Bangladeshi degrees in global recognition systems. Moreover, economically disadvantaged students who cannot afford premium AI subscriptions like GPT-4 would be systematically disadvantaged, creating a new form of inequality. Studies from the University of Dhaka's education department indicate that over 60% of students report reduced motivation to deeply learn material when AI shortcuts are available. The long-term consequence would be a generation of graduates who are dependent on AI without truly understanding foundational concepts.

topic: Bangladesh should implement a complete ban on single-use plastics
for_argument: Bangladesh must implement an immediate and comprehensive ban on single-use plastics to protect its rivers, coastal ecosystems, and public health. The Buriganga, Turag, and Shitalakhya rivers—vital to millions of Bangladeshis—are among the most polluted in the world, with plastic waste being a primary contributor. The Bangladesh River Research Institute documented over 200,000 tonnes of plastic entering waterways annually, directly causing fish mortality rates to increase by 35% over the past decade. Single-use plastics also clog drainage systems, directly worsening urban flooding in Dhaka during monsoon season—a city that already faces catastrophic flooding every year. Bangladesh has already shown it can lead on this issue: it was the first country in the world to ban thin polythene bags in 2002, demonstrating both the political will and the policy infrastructure to act decisively. Jute, Bangladesh's golden fiber, provides an economically superior and already-established alternative for packaging needs, supporting rural livelihoods simultaneously. India, China, and the EU have all moved toward comprehensive plastic bans with measurable environmental improvements, and Bangladesh must not fall behind.
against_argument: A complete ban on single-use plastics in Bangladesh, while environmentally well-intentioned, would create severe economic and social disruptions that the country is not yet equipped to handle. Bangladesh's small-scale plastic manufacturing sector employs approximately 2 million workers, predominantly from low-income backgrounds. An abrupt ban without an adequate transition period and economic safety nets would result in mass unemployment and push vulnerable families deeper into poverty. The practical alternatives to single-use plastics—biodegradable packaging, jute products, glass containers—are currently 3 to 5 times more expensive, making them inaccessible to the majority of Bangladeshi consumers who live on limited daily budgets. Furthermore, the cold chain and food safety infrastructure in Bangladesh relies heavily on plastic packaging to prevent food spoilage and contamination; eliminating it without equivalent alternatives risks significant food waste and public health consequences. The enforcement mechanism for such a ban is also deeply problematic: Bangladesh's regulatory capacity is stretched thin, and the 2002 polythene bag ban is widely acknowledged to have failed due to poor enforcement, with plastic bags still ubiquitous across markets. A phased reduction strategy combined with investment in alternative industries would be far more effective and equitable.

topic: Dhaka should transition to an electric public transport system within 10 years
for_argument: Dhaka's transition to a fully electric public transport system within a decade is not only feasible but urgently necessary for the health, economy, and future of Bangladesh's capital. Dhaka consistently ranks among the world's most polluted cities; the World Health Organization estimates that air pollution causes over 80,000 premature deaths annually in Bangladesh, with transport emissions accounting for 30% of Dhaka's particulate matter. Electric buses and trains produce zero direct emissions, and as Bangladesh's electricity grid increasingly incorporates renewable sources—solar capacity has grown 300% since 2015—the indirect carbon footprint continues to shrink. The economic case is equally compelling: electric vehicles have 60-80% lower operating costs than diesel alternatives due to cheaper electricity, fewer moving parts, and reduced maintenance. Dhaka's existing Metrorail project, which runs on electricity, has already demonstrated that Bangladeshi commuters readily adopt modern electric transit when it is reliable, comfortable, and affordable. Crucially, China successfully transitioned Shenzhen's entire 16,000-bus fleet to electric in just 5 years, proving the timeline is achievable with the right policy and investment.
against_argument: The proposal to transition Dhaka's entire public transport system to electric within 10 years drastically underestimates the infrastructural, financial, and social complexities involved. Electric vehicles require a robust and reliable electricity grid—something Dhaka has historically struggled with, experiencing frequent load-shedding that would render electric buses inoperable during critical commuting hours. The upfront capital cost of replacing Dhaka's transport fleet is enormous; electric buses cost approximately $200,000-$300,000 each, and replacing even 5,000 buses would require $1-1.5 billion in investment, not accounting for charging infrastructure. Bangladesh's government already faces significant fiscal constraints and debt obligations, making such capital-intensive investment highly risky without substantial foreign aid. The transition would also severely displace the hundreds of thousands of workers dependent on the existing CNG rickshaw, bus, and human-powered transport ecosystem—people with no alternative employment and no social safety net. Furthermore, the battery supply chain for electric vehicles depends heavily on lithium imports from South America and cobalt from Congo, creating new geopolitical dependencies that expose Bangladesh to supply chain vulnerabilities it currently does not face with domestic CNG.
